coredata.dc:title: Improvement of the current generation in silicon solar cells by utilizing graphene quantum dot as spectral converter
coredata.dc:creator: Sabetghadam, S.A.
Hosseini, Z.
Zarei, S.
Ghanbari, T.
coredata.dc:description: This paper deals with a luminescent layer applied on a commercial Silicon (Si) solar cell by spin coating of an aqueous solution of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), synthesized by the hydrothermal method at 200 °C for 24 h. The GQDs layer, acted as a luminescent down-shifting (LDS) layer, causes a relative enhancement in the short circuit current density (JSC) of mc-Si and c-Si solar cells up to 5.14% and 6.82%, respectively. Remarkable relative enhancements in JSC under UV illumination showed that the LDS layer increases the JSC by downshifting effect; Diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) of the solar cells with and without the LDS layers showed that the GQD filled LDS layers do not have a considerable effect on the light reflection from the Si solar cell to affect the JSC.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon materials
Luminescence
Solar energy materials

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterizations of GQDs synthesized by hydrothermal method at 200 °C for 24 h. (a) TEM image of GQDs. (b) XRD pattern of GQDs (c) Normalized absorption and emission spectra of GQDs.

J-V curves of Si solar cells with different numbers of spin-coated GQDs layers as the LDS layers compared to a reference Si solar cell without the LDS layer (NL 0), measured under standard AM1.5G one-sun irradiation (100 mW cm−2), (a) mc-Si solar cells and (b) c-Si solar cells. ΔJSC versus the number of GQD layers for (c) mc-Si solar cells and (d) c-Si solar cells, under the illumination of UV lamp along with the ΔJSC obtained from the J-V measurements under illumination of AM1.5G spectrum. NL stands for the number of layers.
Diffuse reflectance spectra of Si solar cells with GQDs layer as the LDS layer compared to the reference Si solar cell without the LDS layer (a) mc-Si Solar cell, (b) c-Si Solar cell.
Photovoltaic parameters of Si solar cells with different numbers of spin-coated GQDs layers as the LDS layer compared to the reference Si solar cell without the LDS layer.

head.ce:title: Improvement of the current generation in silicon solar cells by utilizing graphene quantum dot as spectral converter

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Advanced Technologies, Shiraz University, Shiraz 71946-84560, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Advanced Technologies, Shiraz University, Shiraz 71946-84560, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper deals with a luminescent layer applied on a commercial Silicon (Si) solar cell by spin coating of an aqueous solution of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), synthesized by the hydrothermal method at 200 °C for 24 h. The GQDs layer, acted as a luminescent down-shifting (LDS) layer, causes a relative enhancement in the short circuit current density (JSC) of mc-Si and c-Si solar cells up to 5.14% and 6.82%, respectively. Remarkable relative enhancements in JSC under UV illumination showed that the LDS layer increases the JSC by downshifting effect; Diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) of the solar cells with and without the LDS layers showed that the GQD filled LDS layers do not have a considerable effect on the light reflection from the Si solar cell to affect the JSC.

ce:section.ce:para: Si solar cells have the best performance among the photovoltaic systems and are the market leaders in the photovoltaics (PV) industry. They exhibit a high spectral response in the visible and near-infrared (NIR) regions, but an inefficient performance in the ultraviolet (UV)-blue region because of shallow penetration depth of UV-blue irradiation and high optical reflectance [1,2]. Therefore, a challenging approach for improving the efficiency of Si solar cells is to strengthen the UV-blue responsivity for utilizing more high-energy photons in the photocurrent generation. The application of LDS layer has been proposed to improve the spectral response in different kinds of solar cells [3–7]. By using a suitable LDS material which absorbs photons in shorter wavelength regions and then emits photons in longer wavelength regions, where Si solar cell has the best spectral response, the Si solar cell spectral response and thereby the photocurrent will be improved. For this purpose, a luminescent material should realize some specific requirements to maximize the benefits achieved by application of this strategy, discussed in detail in [5].

Luminescent GQDs are large-band-gap semiconductors that have recently received enormous attention because of their unique chemical, electronic, and optical properties [8]. The photon downshifting property of GQDs, is the most intriguing feature for spectral conversion applications in photovoltaics [7,9]. Herein, we discuss the application of GQD layers on Si solar cells by spin coating of an aqueous solution of hydrothermally synthesized GQDs, and investigate the role of this layer in the spectral response of the devices.

N doped GQDs were prepared using the hydrothermal method by a slightly modified procedure reported in [8]. The commercial multi-crystalline silicon (mc-Si) and crystalline silicon (c-Si) solar modules were first cut into 1.5 × 1.5 cm2 cells by laser and then used for the experiments. Deposition of GQD layers on Si solar cells was done by spin-coating of a GQD solution, at 1000 rpm for 60 s and rising time of 10 s. The details of GQD synthesis and all characterizations are explained in Supporting information (SI).

Heating temperature and heating time are two parameters most affecting the final characteristics of the synthesized particles in the hydrothermal process. Herein, different heating temperatures (160 °C, 180 °C, 200 °C) and heating times (8, 10, 12, 24 h) were used in the hydrothermal process for the synthesis of GQDs in order to choose the desirable values of these parameters by monitoring the PL intensity of the GQDs. Since the GQDs are going to be used as the luminescent species in LDS layers, the PL intensity of these particles plays an important role in the performance of this layer. Fig. SI1 shows the PL spectra of the GQDs prepared by hydrothermal treatment at different temperatures (Fig. SI1a) and different times (Fig. SI1b). The PL intensity increases by increasing the heating temperature and heating time and the highest PL intensity is emitted by the GQDs synthesized at 200 °C for 24 h. Therefore, 200 °C and 24 h were selected as the desirable temperature and the desirable time in the hydrothermal synthesis and used in the following experiments. It is worth mentioning that we couldn't do the experiments at higher temperatures or longer times due to the autoclave and furnace safety considerations.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image and X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the GQDs are depicted in Fig. 1 a and b, respectively. The GQDs are smaller than 50 nm (more TEM images are provided in Fig SI2) and a broad diffraction peak is observed in their XRD pattern at around 2
θ
= 25 corresponding to (002) crystal phase of graphite. The XRD pattern also exhibits a broad peak around 2
θ
= 10 corresponding to the (001) plane of graphitic nitride carbon [10].

The GQD serves as the LDS material in the present work. Fig. 1c shows the normalized absorption and emission spectra of the GQDs. The absorption range of the GQDs fully covers the short-wavelength part of the spectrum, in which both mc-Si and c-Si solar cells usually show a low spectral response. Consequently, the solar irradiation below 500 nm can be absorbed by the GQDs, and via the luminescence of GQDs in larger wavelengths, the light-harvesting efficiency and thereby the JSC of Si solar cells will be improved, significantly.

Fig. 2 a and b show the J-V curves of mc-Si and c-Si solar cells with different numbers of spin-coated GQDs layers as the LDS layer, measured under standard AM1.5G one-sun irradiation (100 mW cm−2); the corresponding photovoltaic parameters are shown in Table 1 . For both mc-Si and c-Si solar cells, the devices with the LDS layers have much greater JSC than the reference devices without the LDS layer. Since the LDS layer is applied outside the solar cells, it affects the light-harvesting efficiency and thereby the JSC of the solar cells. However, it does not change the open-circuit voltage (VOC) and the fill factor (FF) of the devices, significantly. In Table 1, we also reported ΔJSC/%, defined as (JSC,LDS − JSC,bare)/JSC,bare; JSC,LDS and JSC,bare represent the JSC of devices with and without the LDS layer, respectively. The JSC of Si solar cell with the LDS layer first increases and then decreases after deposition of an optimum number of GQD layers, which is 4 and 3 for mc-Si and c-Si solar cells, respectively. Applying the optimum number of layers, ΔJSC/% attained 5.14 and 6.82 for mc-Si and c-Si solar cells, respectively. Several factors affect the choice of the thickness of the LDS layer. A comprehensive discussion on these factors is available in [5]. The responsible factor here is parasitic absorption (PA); i.e. the amount of overlap between the absorption bands of the LDS layer and the Si solar cell. Since the PA is higher for c-Si solar cell, the optimum thickness of the LDS layer on this solar cell is lower than the mc-Si solar cell.

To determine the responsible mechanism for the enhancement in JSC of the Si solar cells, we also measured the JSC under UV illumination. The emission spectrum of UV lamp (Hitachi, 8 W) is depicted in Fig. SI3. Fig. 2c and 2d show the ΔJSC versus the number of GQD layers for both mc-Si and c-Si solar cells under the illumination of UV lamp along with the ΔJSC obtained from the J-V measurements under illumination of AM1.5G spectrum. The ΔJSC results exhibit prominent enhancements when illuminated by UV photons coinciding with the absorption band of the GQD, experimentally proving the concept of LDS applied herein. Under UV illumination, the highest ΔJSC obtained when the number of GQD layers is 4 and 3 for mc-Si and c-Si solar cells respectively, which is in agreement with the ΔJSC under illumination of AM1.5G spectrum. Although the intensity of UV lamp on the solar cells (around 2 mW cm−2) is less than the intensity of UV photons in AM1.5G spectrum (around 5 mW cm−2), the ΔJSC is much higher under the illumination of UV lamp. This result confirms that herein, the luminescent downshifting of UV to visible photons is the responsible mechanism for the increase in JSC of both mc-Si and c-Si solar cells. Diffuse reflectance spectroscopy of the Si solar cells with and without the LDS layers showed that the GQD filled LDS layers do not have a considerable effect on the light reflection from the mc-Si or c-Si solar cells (Fig. 3 ). Therefore, the enhancement in JSC cannot be due to the anti-reflection property of the GQDs layer.

We presented the application of a GQDs layer as the LDS layer on commercial Si solar cell. The GQDs were synthesized by the hydrothermal method and simply spin-coated on the solar cells. The GQD layer improved the light-harvesting efficiency by down-shifting of the incident UV photons. Accordingly, JSC increased by 5.14% and 6.82% for mc-Si and c-Si solar cells, respectively. To the best of our knowledge, these are the highest amount of ΔJSC reported in the literature for mc-Si and c-Si solar cells caused solely by down-shifting effect of a GQD filled LDS layer.

S.A. Sabetghadam: Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Visualization. Z. Hosseini: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Writing - review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. S. Zarei: Investigation, Visualization. T. Ghanbari: Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2020.128515.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bandgap-tuned CuInS2/ZnS core/shell quantum dots for a luminescent downshifting layer in a crystalline silicon solar module
ACS Appl. Nano Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Nakamura, Y. Iso, T. Isobe, Bandgap-Tuned CuInS2/ZnS Core/Shell Quantum Dots for a Luminescent Downshifting Layer in a Crystalline Silicon Solar Module, ACS Appl. Nano Mater. 3 (2020) 3417–3426. doi:10.1021/acsanm.0c00175.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Improving the efficiency of silicon solar cells using in situ fabricated perovskite quantum dots as luminescence downshifting materials
Nanophotonics

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Meng, X.-G. Wu, S. Ma, L. Shi, M. Zhang, L. Wang, Y. Chen, Q. Chen, H. Zhong, Improving the efficiency of silicon solar cells using in situ fabricated perovskite quantum dots as luminescence downshifting materials, Nanophotonics. 9 (2020) 93–100. doi:10.1515/nanoph-2019-0320.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Application of a dual functional luminescent layer to enhance the light harvesting efficiency of dye sensitized solar cell
Mater. Lett.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Hosseini, N. Taghavinia, E.W.-G. Diau, Application of a dual functional luminescent layer to enhance the light harvesting efficiency of dye sensitized solar cell, Mater. Lett. 188 (2017) 92–94. doi:10.1016/j.matlet.2016.10.104.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Luminescent down-shifting CsPbBr 3 perovskite nanocrystals for flexible Cu(In,Ga)Se 2 solar cells
Nanoscale

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.C. Kim, H.J. Jeong, S.T. Kim, Y.H. Song, B.Y. Kim, J.P. Kim, B.K. Kang, J.H. Yun, J.H. Jang, Luminescent down-shifting CsPbBr3 perovskite nanocrystals for flexible Cu(In,Ga)Se2 solar cells, Nanoscale. 12 (2020) 558–562. doi:10.1039/c9nr06041c.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Designing an efficient graphene quantum dot-filled luminescent down shifting layer to improve the stability and efficiency of perovskite solar cells by simple optical modeling
RSC Adv.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Hosseini, T. Ghanbari, Designing an efficient graphene quantum dot-filled luminescent down shifting layer to improve the stability and efficiency of perovskite solar cells by simple optical modeling, RSC Adv. 8 (2018) 31502–31509. doi:10.1039/C8RA06196C.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Luminescent down-shifting natural dyes to enhance photovoltaic efficiency of multicrystalline silicon solar module
Solar Energy

Nano Lett.

He, Efficiency Enhancement of Silicon Heterojunction Solar Cells via Photon Management Using Graphene Quantum Dot as Downconverters, Nano Lett. 16 (2016) 309–313. doi:10.1021/acs.nanolett.5b03814.

Sci Rep

ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces
